title: The two-level systems in cryogenic solids, or how to avoid stressful memories
abstract: Structural glasses prepared by bulk quenching a liquid melt universally exhibit puzzling low-energy excitations commonly known as the ``two-level systems'' (TLSs). Recent studies indicate that ultrastable glassy films made by vapor deposition exhibit substantially fewer TLSs and, at the same time, are more stable enthalpically than conventional glasses made by quenching a melt. A similar phenomenon is observed in very stable glasses of model liquid mixtures prepared using swap Monte Carlo sampling. However, in a separate set of enthalpically stable solids, exemplified by amber matured over geological times, the two-level systems persist. In addressing this seeming conflict, we emphasize that a depletion of the TLSs, if any, means the configurational entropy of the material is lower than that of conventional glasses made by bulk-quenching a melt. Ageing does induce reduction in configurational entropy, but amber, we speculate, achieves enthalpic stabilization through increased bonding, not ageing. We separately comment on the discrepancy among existing predictions for the extent of cooperativity of the two-level systems. Several experiments are suggested to test the present picture.
summary: This paper investigates the origin of low-energy excitations (two-level systems) in various glassy states. It addresses why certain highly stable glasses show a depletion of these excitations while others, like ancient amber, do not. The author argues that TLS depletion is fundamentally tied to a decrease in configurational entropy. By distinguishing between stability gained through entropy reduction (ageing) and stability gained through increased bonding (compression), the paper provides a unified view of how preparation history dictates the density of low-temperature anomalies in disordered solids.
main_problem: Resolving the conflict regarding why two-level systems (TLSs) are depleted in ultrastable glasses and supercooled liquids but persist in other enthalpically stable solids like aged amber.
main_result: The author proposes that TLS depletion is linked to a reduction in configurational entropy, whereas the stability in amber arises from increased bonding (compression) rather than entropy reduction via ageing.
method: Theoretical analysis using Random First Order Transition (RFOT) theory, thermodynamic analysis of enthalpy and entropy, and a toy model Hamiltonian representing a two-state memory unit.
model_or_system: The study focuses on structural glasses, including bulk-quenched melts, vapor-deposited ultrastable films, swap Monte Carlo-stabilized Lennard-Jones mixtures, and geologically aged amber.
key_observables: Two-level system (TLS) density of states, configurational entropy, enthalpy, heat capacity, and cooperativity size.
important_parameters: Glass transition temperature (Tg), configurational entropy (sc), cooperativity length (xi), and the mismatch penalty (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The speculation that amber's stability is driven by polymerization-induced compression rather than ageing-induced entropy reduction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows an equation of state diagram for enthalpy vs. entropy; Figure 2 shows signatures of TLS depletion in heat capacity; Figure 4 depicts enthalpy vs. entropy for ordered phases; Figure 5 shows temperature dependence of transition-state size; Figure 6 illustrates density and entropy changes during ageing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the TLS discrepancy, develops a thermodynamic framework linking TLS density to configurational entropy, analyzes ultrastable glasses and swap-MC liquids, addresses the specific case of amber via a compression hypothesis, and concludes with implications for TLS manipulation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The equilibrium equation of state (EOS) of a substance in the enthalpy range pertinent to liquid- crystal coexistence. The thick blue line depicts the liquid entropy as a function of enthalpy, the thick black line the entropy of the crystal. The solid portion of the liquid EOS corresponds to the ergodic regime T > Tcr, the dashed portion signiﬁes ergodicity is broken on times less than the structural relaxation time τα. The states between HXtal and Hliq are bypassed during quasi-equilibrium crystallisation but are visited, if the liquid can be supercooled below the melting temperature. The glass transition ordinarily occurs at enthalpy values within the enthalpy gap [HXtal, Hliq]....
Figure 2. Signatures of depletion in the number of two-level systems in (a) ultrastable glasses [21] and (b) model Lennard-Jones mixtures [22].

Figure 3. Graphical explanation of ergodicity breaking. p(x) stands for the probability distribution of the variable x. Note that we have not included the additive contribution −Tsvib of the vibrational entropy to the free energies G1, G2, and G, for clarity. This contribution is the same for all three free energies.

Figure 4. Main graph (left): Sketch of the enthalpy dependence of the entropy for a liquid that allows for two low-enthalpy phases that exhibit local ordering; these two phases are labelled “ordered” USG. “USG”=“ultrastable glass”. “EOS”=“equilibrium equation of state.” The corresponding down-scan (blue dashed) and up-scan for DSC are sketched in the auxiliary graph on the right. Note the respective slopes of the two double-tangent lines in the main graph should be much closer to each other, but were drawn this way to avoid cluttering.
Figure 5. Temperature dependence of the degree cooperativity for (a) α-relaxations and (b) β-relaxation. From Ref. [22].
Figure 6. Diagrams juxtaposing equilibrium and oﬀ-equilibrium behaviours of a liquid.
